overview.body: You've built an amazing road trip game over Module 2 — full validation, multiple legs, session statistics across multiple games. But there's one huge problem: everything vanishes when you quit! Imagine playing for 20 minutes, racking up three games of session stats, then accidentally closing the program. All that progress — gone. This week, you'll learn to save your full session to a file and load it back when you return. Welcome to file I/O — the skill that makes data persist!

mentorQuote.quote: Module 2 was about logic. Module 3 is about persistence. Once your program can save state to disk and load it back, you cross a line. You stop writing scripts and start writing real software.

objectives: A save_game() function that writes 9 fields to a file (your full session state)
A load_game() function that reads the file back, returns a 9-tuple, and handles missing files
A main menu: New Game / Load Saved Game / Quit
Resume logic that picks up a saved session mid-trip and keeps session totals intact
A 3-way mid-trip option: Continue / Save and quit / Quit without saving
overview.keyConceptsText: File I/O with open(), write(), readline(), and close(). Exception handling with try-except FileNotFoundError. Type conversion between strings and integers when crossing the file boundary.
programRequirements: Your program MUST use open() with 'w' mode to write the save file
Your program MUST use open() with 'r' mode to read the save file
Your program MUST use try-except FileNotFoundError when loading
Your program MUST convert numbers to strings with str() before writing
Your program MUST use rstrip('\n') when reading lines
Your program MUST save and restore all 9 fields (per-game state + session accumulators)
overview.filenameNote: Save your file as lab-3-1.py. Your function_library.py from Lab 2.3 carries forward unchanged.
overview.additionalFilesNote: Submit both lab-3-1.py and function_library.py. The save file savegame.txt is generated at runtime; you do not submit it.

dataFiles.format: Your reusable function module from Lab 2.3, unchanged. 5 functions: flip_coin(), roll_d20(), check_percentage(), get_valid_int(), get_yes_no().

rows.task: Copy lab-2-3.py to lab-3-1.py and inventory what carries forward
Write the read function with try-except
Wrap the game with a New/Load/Quit menu
Unpack the loaded tuple and route into the game
Run end-to-end test, verify expected output, submit to CodeGrade

beforeYouBegin.intro: Here's how your finished program fits together. You're wrapping your Lab 2.3 game with two new layers: a save/load system that persists state to disk, and a main menu that ties it all together. The [NEW] tags show what's new this week. (keep from 2.3) marks the chunks that carry forward unchanged.
beforeYouBegin.architecture:
# ---- CONSTANTS ----
SAT_TO_CORPUS = 150            (keep from 2.3)
SAT_TO_HOUSTON = 200           (keep from 2.3)
SAT_TO_AUSTIN = 80             (keep from 2.3)
HOU_TO_GALVESTON = 50          (keep from 2.3)
HOU_TO_SAN_ANTONIO = 200       (keep from 2.3)
MILES_MULTIPLIER = 10          (keep from 2.3)
HAZARD_CHANCE = 30             (keep from 2.3)
SAVE_FILE = 'savegame.txt'     [NEW-CP1]

# ---- FUNCTIONS ----
def save_game(player_name, current_city, total_miles, total_turns,
              total_hazards, total_escaped, games_played,
              session_miles, session_hazards):    [NEW-CP2]
│── Open SAVE_FILE in 'w' mode
│── Write each parameter on its own line
│── Convert integers with str() before writing
│── Close file
│── Print 'Game saved!'

def load_game():                                  [NEW-CP3]
│── try: open SAVE_FILE in 'r' mode
│    ├── Read 9 lines with readline().rstrip('\n')
│    ├── Convert numeric lines with int()
│    └── Return 9-tuple
│── except FileNotFoundError:
│    └── Return None

# ---- MAIN MENU LOOP ----   [NEW-CP4]
while in_program:
│── Display menu (1=New, 2=Load, 3=Quit)
│── main_choice = get_valid_int('Choice (1-3): ', 1, 3)
│
│── if main_choice == 3:
│    └── in_program = False, break out
│
│── if main_choice == 2:    [NEW-CP5]
│    ├── saved = load_game()
│    ├── if saved is None: print 'No saved game found.', fall to New Game
│    └── else: unpack 9-tuple, set resumed_game = True
│
│── if main_choice == 1:
│    ├── Get player_name from input
│    └── Initialize session accumulators to 0
│
│── # ---- PLAY-AGAIN LOOP ----   (keep from 2.3)
│   play_again = True
│   while play_again:
│   │
│   │── if not resumed_game: reset per-game state
│   │── resumed_game = False
│   │
│   │── # ---- IN-GAME LOOP ----   (keep from 2.3)
│   │   while playing:
│   │   │── Menu → Destination Gate → Travel Loop → Hazards
│   │   │
│   │   │── If beach city: playing = False, reached_beach = True
│   │   │── If Austin: ask Return to San Antonio?
│   │   │── If Houston/SA: 3-way prompt   [REPLACED-CP6]
│   │       │── 1. Continue journey
│   │       │── 2. Save and quit  → save_game(), saved_and_quit = True
│   │       └── 3. Quit without saving → playing = False
│   │
│   │── After in-game loop:
│   │   ├── if saved_and_quit: play_again = False, continue (back to main menu)
│   │   ├── Print TRIP SUMMARY
│   │   ├── Update session accumulators
│   │   └── play_again = get_yes_no('Play again? (y/n): ')
│   │
│   └── After play-again loop:
│       └── if not saved_and_quit: Print SESSION STATISTICS
│
└── Back to main menu loop

content.quote: Project handoff time. Lab 2.3 is a complete, working game. What we are doing this week is wrapping it with a save/load layer and a main menu. The game logic itself does not change. Before you write a single new line of code, let's be explicit about what carries forward, what gets renamed, and what's brand new. Five minutes of inventory now saves an hour of debugging later.

content.body: A clean starting point for Lab 3.1. You'll copy lab-2-3.py to lab-3-1.py, update the docstring, and confirm everything still runs before you add anything new.

Mixing 'fix the old code' with 'add new code' is the fastest way to break a working program. Get the carryover right first, run it once to confirm it still works, then start adding the new features in clean steps. Every checkpoint after this one assumes you started from a working Lab 2.3.

items: Copy lab-2-3.py to a new file named lab-3-1.py in the same folder
Update the docstring at the top: change the title to Lab 3.1: Save Your Journey and update the description and chapter list
Do NOT change function_library.py — it carries forward unchanged this week
Run your new lab-3-1.py exactly as-is. It should behave identically to Lab 2.3. If it does not, fix the difference before moving on
Review the inventory tables below so you know exactly what you're keeping and what you'll add in CPs 2–6

content.body: No new BookEx patterns yet — this checkpoint is pure cleanup. CP2 introduces Program 6-1 (Writing to a File) and CP3 introduces Programs 6-3 and 6-28 (reading with try-except).

'Play again? (y/n): ', 'Keep going this trip? (y/n): ', 'Return to San Antonio? (y/n): '
Displays
TRIP SUMMARY block (per game). SESSION STATISTICS block (per session).

content.body: Run your fresh lab-3-1.py end-to-end exactly as-is. Play one game, decline replay, watch session stats print. If anything behaves differently from Lab 2.3, you have a copy problem — fix it before CP2.

rows: Editing lab-2-3.py in place instead of copying it
Always preserve the previous lab's submission. Copy first, then edit the copy.
Touching function_library.py
Module 3.1 makes no changes to the library. The 4 new file-handling functions land in Lab 3.2, not here.
Forgetting to update the docstring at the top
CodeGrade and your instructor read the docstring to identify the submission. Change the title and description, not just the filename.

items: A file named lab-3-1.py in your project folder
Updated docstring at the top of lab-3-1.py
function_library.py unchanged
Program runs and plays identically to Lab 2.3

content.body: A function that takes 9 parameters (2 strings + 7 integers), opens savegame.txt in write mode, and writes each parameter on its own line.

This is your first time making data persist beyond a single program run. The file is plain text, so you can open it in Notepad to inspect what you wrote. Two rules of file writing: write() does not add newlines automatically (you must concatenate '\n'), and write() only accepts strings (you must str() any integers).

content.quote: Think about how this feels to the player. They play three games, build up to 1200 session miles, then have to step away. When they come back tomorrow and load their save, that 1200 has to still be there. Otherwise the entire session-stats layer we built in Lab 2.3 evaporates the moment they close the program. The 9 fields are not arbitrary — they are the complete picture of who this player is right now.

items: Add a constant near the top of lab-3-1.py: SAVE_FILE = 'savegame.txt'
Define save_game() with 9 parameters (see the code skeleton below for the exact signature)
Open the file for writing: outfile = open(SAVE_FILE, 'w')
Write each parameter on its own line. Strings get outfile.write(name + '\n'); integers need outfile.write(str(value) + '\n')
Write all 9 fields in this exact order: player_name, current_city, total_miles, total_turns, total_hazards, total_escaped, games_played, session_miles, session_hazards
Close the file: outfile.close()
Print confirmation: print('Game saved!')

content.code:
def save_game(player_name, current_city, total_miles, total_turns,
              total_hazards, total_escaped, games_played,
              session_miles, session_hazards):
    """Save full session state to a file."""
    # 1. Open SAVE_FILE in 'w' mode
    # 2. Write each parameter on its own line (with '\n')
    # 3. Convert integers to strings with str() before writing
    # 4. Close the file
    # 5. Print 'Game saved!'
    pass

content.body: Program 6-1 (file_write.py): the basic open-write-close pattern. Program 6-4 (write_names.py): writing variables with '\n' concatenation. Program 6-7 (write_numbers.py): using str() to convert numbers to strings before write().
Step 1: Add this test harness at the END of your file (after all function definitions):

content.code:
# DEBUG - DELETE BEFORE CHECKPOINT 3
save_game('TestPlayer', 'Houston', 350, 5, 3, 2,
          1, 450, 4)
print('Check savegame.txt in your folder!')

content.body: Step 2: Run the program. Open savegame.txt in Notepad. Verify these 9 lines (one value per line, in this exact order):
Step 3: Verify each value is on its OWN line (9 lines total, not 1 long line). If you see everything on one line, you forgot the '\n'.
Step 4: DELETE the test harness lines before moving to CP3.

rows: Forgetting '\n'
Everything ends up on one line. Each write() needs + '\n'.
Writing integers without str()
TypeError: write() argument must be str. Convert with str(total_miles).
Wrong field order
load_game() reads in the same order, so a mismatch here breaks loading. Save in: name, city, then per-game totals (miles, turns, hazards, escaped), then session totals (games, miles, hazards).
Forgetting outfile.close()
On some systems the file is partially written. Always close after writing.

items: SAVE_FILE constant defined near the top of the file
save_game() function with 9 parameters in the documented order
9 write() calls, each with + '\n'
str() conversion on all 7 numeric values
outfile.close() at the end
savegame.txt created with 9 human-readable lines

content.body: A function that reads the 9 lines you wrote in CP2, converts them back to the right types, and returns them as a tuple. If the save file doesn't exist, it returns None instead of crashing.

Reading from disk has two failure modes: the file might not exist (player has never saved), or the data might be the wrong type (integers come back as strings until you int() them). The try-except pattern handles the first; the conversion handles the second. Without both, your program crashes the first time anyone clicks Load.

content.quote: Here is my test plan. First, delete any savegame.txt and call load_game(). It must return None, not crash. Second, save a known game state, then load it. The 9 values you get back must match the 9 you saved exactly. If any integer comes back as a string, the rest of the game will blow up the first time you try math on it. Strings out, ints in, every time.

items: Define load_game() with no parameters (it reads from SAVE_FILE)
Start a try block: try:
Open for reading: infile = open(SAVE_FILE, 'r')
Read the 2 string fields: use infile.readline().rstrip('\n') for player_name and current_city
Read the 7 integer fields: wrap each readline().rstrip('\n') in int(...)
Read in the same order you saved: name, city, total_miles, total_turns, total_hazards, total_escaped, games_played, session_miles, session_hazards
Close the file with infile.close()
Return a 9-tuple with all values in the same order
Add the except clause: except FileNotFoundError: followed by return None

content.code:
def load_game():
    """Load full session state. Returns a 9-tuple, or None if no save exists."""
    try:
        # 1. Open SAVE_FILE in 'r' mode
        # 2. Read 9 lines with readline().rstrip('\n')
        # 3. Convert numeric lines back to int()
        # 4. Close the file
        # 5. Return a 9-tuple of values
        pass
    except FileNotFoundError:
        # No save file exists yet
        return None

content.body: Program 6-3 (line_read.py): reading one line at a time with readline(). Program 6-5 (strip_newline.py): using rstrip('\n') to clean up file input. Program 6-13 (file_with_numbers.py): converting string lines to int. Program 6-28 (display_file2.py): handling missing files with try-except FileNotFoundError.
Step 1: Make sure savegame.txt from CP2 still exists. Add this test harness:
Step 2: Run the program. Verify this output (note: integers, not strings):
Step 3: Delete savegame.txt, run again. Verify: No save file found.
Step 4: DELETE the test harness lines before moving to CP4.

rows: Forgetting rstrip('\n')
Strings still have the trailing newline. current_city becomes 'Houston\n' which won't match 'Houston' in your menu comparisons.
Forgetting int() on numeric reads
Tuple shows '350' with quotes instead of 350. Math operations later will crash or concatenate strings.
Reading in wrong order
Each readline() moves to the next line; if your read order doesn't match your save order, you load total_miles into hazards and so on.
Forgetting return None in the except branch
The function returns None by default if it falls off the end, but being explicit is better — the caller is going to check if saved is None.

items: load_game() function with a try-except block
9 readline() calls, each with rstrip('\n')
int() conversion on the 7 numeric values
Returns a 9-tuple on success (in the same order as save_game() writes)
Returns None when the file doesn't exist

content.body: An outermost while loop that wraps your entire game. Each iteration shows three choices: New Game, Load Saved Game, or Quit. Routing happens here; the actual load logic lives in CP5.

Lab 2.3 already had two nested loops (play-again and in-game). The main menu is a third loop wrapped around both of them. It exists so the player can finish a session, return to the menu, and either start fresh, load an old save, or quit the program entirely. Three loops is a lot — but each one has one job, and as long as you keep them straight the code stays readable.

items: In main(), wrap your entire existing game in a new outermost loop: in_program = True followed by while in_program:
Indent all the existing game code one level so it's inside the new loop
At the top of the loop, display the main menu: 1. New Game, 2. Load Saved Game, 3. Quit
Get the choice: main_choice = function_library.get_valid_int('Choice (1-3): ', 1, 3)
If choice is 3: print 'Thanks for playing!', set in_program = False, use continue to skip back to the top of the loop (which will then exit because in_program is False)
If choice is 1: prompt for the player's name and initialize the session accumulators (games_played = 0, session_miles = 0, session_hazards = 0) and set current_city = 'San Antonio'
If choice is 2: leave a placeholder comment # Resume from saved (CP5). CP5 fills in the load logic

content.body: No new BookEx patterns for this CP. You're applying the validated-input pattern from Lab 2.3 to a third menu and the sentinel-loop pattern from Chapter 4.5 to wrap the whole program. Program 4-1 (commission.py) is still your reference for the sentinel-loop shape.
Step 1: Run your program. Verify the main menu appears before anything else.
Step 2: Choose 3. Verify the program exits cleanly with 'Thanks for playing!'.

Step 3: Run again. Choose 1 (New Game). Verify the game proceeds exactly as it did in Lab 2.3. Finish or decline replay. When the session stats finish printing, you should be back at the main menu, not exited from the program.
Step 4: Choose 2 (Load) right now. Since CP5 isn't done, you'll get something broken — that's fine. Verify the main menu loop is wired correctly even if the load branch is incomplete.

rows: Forgetting to indent the existing game
Python won't know what's inside the new loop. Every line of game code from Lab 2.3 must indent one level.
Returning instead of breaking out of the loop
Using return inside main() works but skips any cleanup. Using in_program = False followed by continue is the sentinel-loop pattern from Chapter 4.5.
Initializing session accumulators in the wrong place
They go inside the if main_choice == 1 branch (or in the Load branch in CP5). NOT before the main menu loop — otherwise quitting and starting a New Game would carry over old totals.

items: main_choice == 3 exits cleanly
main_choice == 1 starts a new game and returns to main menu when done
main_choice == 2 branch exists (placeholder for CP5 is fine)
All Lab 2.3 game logic is indented inside the new main menu loop
Session accumulators initialize fresh on each New Game

content.body: The Load branch from CP4. When the player chooses 2 at the main menu, you call load_game(), check the return value, and either restore the 9 fields or fall back to a new game. You also set a resumed_game flag so the play-again loop knows not to reset per-game state on the first iteration.

Unpacking a 9-tuple cleanly is a real skill. Get the order wrong and your current_city becomes 350. The resumed_game flag is the trickier part: your play-again loop resets per-game state at the top of each iteration. When you resume a saved mid-trip game, you want to KEEP the loaded per-game state, not reset it. One Boolean flag, used once on the first iteration only, threads the difference through cleanly.

items: Before the play-again loop, add resumed_game = False. This will be the default for New Game
In the if main_choice == 2: branch, call saved = load_game()
If saved is None: print 'No saved game found. Starting new game.' and set main_choice = 1 so the New Game branch runs next

Otherwise (saved is a tuple): unpack the 9 values into the same variable names you used in save_game(). Use parallel assignment: (player_name, current_city, total_miles, total_turns, total_hazards, total_escaped, games_played, session_miles, session_hazards) = saved
Print a welcome-back message using the loaded values
Set resumed_game = True so the play-again loop knows not to reset
Inside the play-again loop, change the per-game reset to be conditional: if not resumed_game: then reset, otherwise skip the reset
Set resumed_game = False at the end of the conditional, so only the FIRST iteration resumes from save; subsequent iterations reset normally

content.body: Program 5-13 (return_tuple.py): a function that returns multiple values via a tuple, unpacked at the call site. Program 4-21 (retail_with_validation.py): setting a Boolean flag based on a condition and using it later to control flow.
Step 1: Delete savegame.txt if it exists. Run the program, choose 2 (Load). Verify you see 'No saved game found. Starting new game.' and the New Game flow runs.
Step 2: Once CP6 is done you'll be able to create a real save mid-game. For now, manually create savegame.txt with these 9 lines (replace with any test values):
Step 3: Run the program, choose 2. Verify the welcome-back message names the player and the city, and that the game proceeds from Houston (not from San Antonio).

rows: Unpacking in the wrong order
The order MUST match the tuple your load_game() returns, which MUST match the order save_game() writes. All three places use the same order. If you change one, change all three.
Forgetting resumed_game = False after the first iteration
If you resume, then play again, the second game starts with leftover total_miles from the resumed game. The flag must reset itself after the first use.
Wrapping the whole per-game reset in if not resumed_game: but forgetting one variable
Easy mistake. The cleanest pattern is one if with all six per-game resets inside it: total_miles, total_turns, total_hazards, total_escaped, reached_beach, saved_and_quit.
Trying to handle resume mid-game when current_city is a beach city
The solution treats this as 'you saved at victory; replay the celebration.' Don't write extra branches for edge cases your save format can't actually produce.

items: main_choice == 2 calls load_game()
None return falls back to New Game cleanly
Successful load unpacks all 9 fields by name
Welcome-back message uses the loaded values
resumed_game flag prevents per-game reset on the first iteration only

content.body: Replace the existing 'Keep going this trip? (y/n): ' prompt with a 3-way choice: 1. Continue journey, 2. Save and quit, 3. Quit without saving. Save & quit calls save_game() and exits all the way back to the main menu, skipping the trip summary.

Save & quit is not the same as 'finish the game.' When the player saves mid-trip, the game is paused, not over. They don't want to see a TRIP SUMMARY or SESSION STATISTICS that suggests the game is done; they want to go back to the main menu so they can quit or pick up later. The saved_and_quit flag is how you skip the end-of-game displays cleanly.

content.quote: Real talk: I shipped a save feature on a side project once where 'Save and quit' fell through to the trip summary. Players saw 'Status: Trip Incomplete' even though they had just saved a perfectly fine game in progress. They thought the save had failed. I had to patch it within a week. The fix is exactly what we are doing here: a flag that says 'this was a save, not an end-of-game,' and we honor it in the post-loop code. Two extra lines, no confused players.

items: Inside the play-again loop, add saved_and_quit = False alongside the other per-game state
Find the existing get_yes_no('Keep going this trip? (y/n): ') in the Houston/SA branch of the in-game loop
Replace it with a printed 3-option menu and a get_valid_int call: opt = function_library.get_valid_int('Choice (1-3): ', 1, 3)
If opt == 2: call save_game() with all 9 current values, set saved_and_quit = True, set playing = False
If opt == 3: set quit_without_saving = True and playing = False
If opt == 1: do nothing — the loop continues naturally
After the in-game loop ends, check if saved_and_quit: and if so, set play_again = False and continue to jump back to the main menu, skipping TRIP SUMMARY and the play-again prompt entirely
After the play-again loop ends, wrap the SESSION STATISTICS print in if not saved_and_quit: so a saved session doesn't print closing stats

content.body: No new BookEx patterns. You're applying the validated-input pattern from Lab 2.3 to a third menu and using Boolean flags to control flow (same pattern as play_again and playing).
Step 1: Start a new game. Travel to Houston (any path). Verify the 3-option menu appears instead of the old yes/no prompt.
Step 2: Choose 2 (Save and quit). Verify you see 'Game saved!' and then the main menu appears immediately — no TRIP SUMMARY, no SESSION STATISTICS.
Step 3: Open savegame.txt. Verify it shows YOUR player name, Houston as the current city, and your real mid-trip totals (not zeros).
Step 4: From the main menu, choose 2 (Load). Verify you resume exactly where you left off and can finish the game normally.

Step 5: Start another new game, travel to Houston, choose 3 (Quit without saving). Verify you see a TRIP SUMMARY with status 'Quit without saving' and then the play-again prompt — this branch DOES print the trip summary.

rows: Save & quit shows trip summary anyway
You forgot the if saved_and_quit: guard after the in-game loop, OR you forgot to continue back to the main menu (instead falling through to the trip summary).
Quit without saving acts the same as Save & quit
The quit_without_saving branch should NOT call save_game() and SHOULD print the trip summary. The two branches are different paths.
Forgetting to pass all 9 args to save_game()
Python error missing N required positional arguments. The call site is long — line it up carefully and verify all 9 are present.
Wrapping the wrong block in if not saved_and_quit:
The TRIP SUMMARY and SESSION STATISTICS are both gated, but at different levels: TRIP SUMMARY is gated inside the play-again loop, SESSION STATISTICS is gated after the play-again loop ends.

items: 3-option menu replaces the old get_yes_no at non-beach cities
Option 2 saves and exits straight to the main menu (no summary, no session stats)
Option 3 quits without saving and DOES print trip summary
Option 1 continues the journey as before
Full save/load cycle works end-to-end

beforeYouSubmit: lab-3-1.py docstring updated with name, abc123, section, and description
Main menu loop wraps the entire game
Resume logic restores all 9 fields, including session accumulators
3-way mid-trip prompt routes Continue / Save & quit / Quit without saving correctly
Save & quit returns to main menu without printing TRIP SUMMARY or SESSION STATISTICS
finalChecklist.scopeReminder: Chapters 2–6 only. CodeGrade will flag code using techniques from later chapters.

specific: All previous techniques (variables, if/else, loops, functions, function_library)
Functions that return tuples; unpacking at the call site
permittedTechniques.notPermitted: lists, dictionaries, with statement, readlines(), append mode, or any Chapter 7+ techniques

finalChecklist.saveYourCode: Keep both files — Lab 3.2 builds on this with lists to track visited cities and expands function_library.py with file-handling helpers.

finalCheck.expectedOutput:
==================================================
DEEP IN THE HEART: A LONE STAR JOURNEY
==================================================

MAIN MENU

finalCheck.expectedOutputNote: This run uses inputs 1 (New Game) → TestPlayer → 2 (Houston) → 1 (Continue journey) → 1 (Galveston) → n (no replay) → 3 (Quit) with the deterministic CodeGrade test fixture. With real random, your specific rolls and hazards will differ — but the structure must match: main menu → new game → play one game → session stats → main menu → quit.

ifSomethingBreaks.redirect: Check CP3 (load_game) — the except FileNotFoundError clause is missing or in the wrong place. Starting a New Game should NOT call load_game(), so this also means the main menu branch in CP4 is calling load_game() for choice 1 by mistake.

ifSomethingBreaks.symptom: After choosing Load, game starts at San Antonio with miles = 0
ifSomethingBreaks.redirect: Check CP5 (Resume) — either the tuple unpacking failed, or the resumed_game flag isn't preventing the per-game reset at the top of the play-again loop. Add a print statement right after unpacking to confirm the loaded values are correct.

ifSomethingBreaks.symptom: Save & quit prints a TRIP SUMMARY even though the game wasn't over
ifSomethingBreaks.redirect: Check CP6 (Mid-Trip Save Option) — the if saved_and_quit: guard after the in-game loop is missing or wraps the wrong code. The TRIP SUMMARY print should be inside the else branch.

ifSomethingBreaks.symptom: All saved values come back as strings (game crashes with TypeError later)
ifSomethingBreaks.redirect: Check CP3 (load_game) — the 7 numeric fields need int() wrapping. Only player_name and current_city stay as strings.

ifSomethingBreaks.symptom: savegame.txt contains all 9 values on a single line
ifSomethingBreaks.redirect: Check CP2 (save_game) — you forgot the + '\n' on each write() call. Each field needs its own newline.

ifSomethingBreaks.symptom: Main menu doesn't reappear after a game finishes
ifSomethingBreaks.redirect: Check CP4 (Main Menu) — the entire game logic must be indented INSIDE the while in_program: loop. If a closing brace got misplaced, the program will run the game once and exit.

ifYouGetStuck: Re-read Programs 6-1 (file_write.py), 6-3 (line_read.py), and 6-28 (display_file2.py) — these three show the exact patterns you need
Test save_game() and load_game() in isolation FIRST with the test harnesses described in CPs 2 and 3, before wiring them into the main game
When debugging the resume logic, add print(f'DEBUG resumed_game={resumed_game}') at the top of the play-again loop
Submit to CodeGrade early — the grader tests save_game() and load_game() separately, so you'll know immediately which function is broken
Come to Zoom or office hours — the three-loop nesting is easier to debug with someone watching the screen
needHelp.bookexResourceNote: Chapter 6 BookEx Reference Guide — step-by-step breakdowns of every textbook example, your primary reference
